title: Cosmic Cliffs — Carina Nebula
desc: Webb’s first look into the Carina Nebula: a coastline of gas and dust seven light-years tall, where hundreds of new stars are igniting.

title: Pillars of Creation
desc: The most famous towers in astronomy, reimagined in Webb’s infrared — newborn stars glow inside columns of gas five light-years tall.

title: Southern Ring Nebula
desc: A dying star’s final act: shells of gas expelled over thousands of years, lit from within by the fading white dwarf at the center.

title: Stephan’s Quintet
desc: Five galaxies locked in a gravitational dance — two of them in mid-collision, sparking waves of new star formation.

title: Webb’s First Deep Field
desc: The deepest infrared view of the universe: thousands of galaxies in a patch of sky the size of a grain of sand at arm’s length.

title: Tarantula Nebula
desc: The most violent star factory in our galactic neighborhood — 161,000 light-years away, home to the most massive stars known.

title: Cartwheel Galaxy
desc: The aftermath of a bullseye: a smaller galaxy punched through this one 400 million years ago, sending rings of star birth outward like ripples.

title: Pillars of Creation (1995–2015)
desc: Hubble’s 2015 return to the Eagle Nebula’s pillars — the image that defined a generation of astronomy.

title: Veil Nebula
desc: Shrapnel from a star that exploded 8,000 years ago, still expanding — a supernova remnant 110 light-years across.

title: Whirlpool Galaxy
desc: M51 and its small companion: the textbook spiral galaxy, its arms compressed into star-forming brilliance by the encounter.

title: Butterfly Nebula
desc: Wings of gas heated to 20,000 degrees, flowing from a dying star at a million kilometers per hour.

title: Hubble Ultra Deep Field
desc: Hubble stared at one dark speck of sky for weeks and found 10,000 galaxies — some of their light older than the Earth itself.

title: Sombrero Galaxy
desc: A galaxy seen edge-on, its dust lane and glowing bulge forming the brim and crown that give M104 its name.

title: Orion Nebula
desc: The nearest great star factory to Earth — visible to your naked eye as the middle “star” in Orion’s sword.

title: Earthrise
desc: The first Earthrise ever witnessed by humans — Apollo 8, Christmas Eve 1968. The photo credited with helping start the environmental movement.

title: Horsehead Nebula
desc: A cosmic seahorse sculpted from a wall of dust, photographed in infrared for Hubble’s 23rd birthday.

title: Crescent Jupiter
desc: Jupiter as a crescent no telescope on Earth can ever see — caught by the Juno spacecraft with the Great Red Spot in view.

title: Antennae Galaxies
desc: Two galaxies caught mid-collision, flinging streams of stars into space. In about four billion years, the Milky Way and Andromeda may look like this.

title: Ring Nebula
desc: A dying sun-like star shedding its outer layers, seen through Webb’s two infrared eyes — a preview of our own Sun’s far future.

title: The Day the Earth Smiled
desc: Cassini photographed Saturn backlit by the Sun — and in the full frame, Earth is a pale blue dot beneath the rings. NASA told the world to look up and smile.

title: Mystic Mountain
desc: A three-light-year pillar in the Carina Nebula, shot for Hubble’s 20th anniversary — infant stars inside fire jets from its peaks.

title: Pale Blue Dot
desc: Voyager 1 turned its camera home from 6 billion kilometers away. That dot in the sunbeam is everyone who ever lived. (2020 remaster of the 1990 original.)

title: Crab Nebula
desc: The wreckage of a supernova that Chinese astronomers watched appear in the year 1054 — still expanding, with a spinning neutron star at its heart.

title: Neptune’s Rings
desc: Neptune glowing in Webb’s infrared — the clearest view of its delicate rings since Voyager 2 flew past in 1989.

title: Bubble Nebula
desc: A single massive star inflating a soap bubble of gas seven light-years wide — big enough to hold our Sun and its nearest neighbor star inside it.

title: Pluto & Charon
desc: Pluto and its moon Charon in true color, from the spacecraft that sprinted nine and a half years to reach them.

title: Lagoon Nebula
desc: The stormy heart of the Lagoon Nebula, carved by the radiation of one giant young star — Hubble’s 28th-birthday portrait.

title: X-Class Solar Flare
desc: Our own star mid-tantrum: an X-class flare — the most powerful class — caught by the observatory that photographs the Sun every 0.75 seconds.

title: Andromeda
desc: A sweep across our neighbor galaxy — the sharpest big-galaxy portrait ever made. The full mosaic resolves 100 million individual stars.
